name: Y et EN — Location & Quantity Pronouns

cards.grammarNote: y replaces à + place (or any location phrase): Je vais au magasin → J'y vais. y is placed before the conjugated verb, just like direct object pronouns.

cards.front: I'm going to the store → I'm going there
cards.back: Je vais au magasin → J'y vais
cards.grammarNote: y replaces à + location. It can also replace à + thing (an idea or concept you are responding to): Tu penses à ta réunion? J'y pense. (Are you thinking about your meeting? I'm thinking about it.)
en replaces de + noun (partitive or quantity context): Tu as du pain? J'en ai. (Do you have bread? I have some.) en goes before the conjugated verb.
en replaces de + place (origin): Je viens du marché → J'en viens. This is the mirror of y (which replaces à + place).
habiter à (to live at/in) → replace à + place with y. J'habite à Lyon → J'y habite.
en replaces du/de la/de l'/des + noun. Tu veux du pain? J'en veux. (Do you want bread? I want some.)

cards.front: She comes from the market → she comes from there
cards.back: Elle vient du marché → Elle en vient

cards.front: How many do you have? — I have three.
cards.back: Tu en as combien ? — J'en ai trois.
cards.grammarNote: en is required even when a number is given: J'en ai trois (I have three of them). The number follows the verb.
In negation, ne wraps before y/en and the verb: je n'y vais plus. ne...plus = no longer / not anymore.
en works with impersonal expressions too: il en reste (there is some left), il n'en reste plus (there is none left).
y can replace à + abstract thing: penser à quelque chose → y penser. J'y pense = I'm thinking about it.
In a negative imperative, y/en stay before the verb: N'y va pas! N'en mange pas! In a positive imperative they follow: Vas-y! Prends-en! (Note: vas adds -s before y for pronunciation.)
